_meta.title: Parser Generators Yacc Bison Lalrpop Tree Sitter

questions.question: What parsing algorithm does Yacc/Bison use by default, and how does it resolve shift-reduce conflicts?
options: LL(1) — conflicts are resolved by always choosing the first production
LALR(1) — conflicts are resolved using precedence/associativity declarations (%left, %right, %prec), with shift winning by default
GLR — conflicts are resolved by forking the parser into all valid paths
PEG — conflicts cannot arise because ordered choice eliminates ambiguity
questions.explanation: Bison uses LALR(1) by default. When a shift-reduce conflict arises (e.g., the dangling-else), Bison resolves it using operator precedence declarations or defaults to shift. The `%left`, `%right`, and `%prec` directives assign precedence levels to terminals and productions, letting Bison pick the correct action.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of tree-sitter's concrete syntax tree (CST) over a traditional abstract syntax tree (AST)?
options: CSTs are smaller and more efficient to store
CSTs preserve all tokens including whitespace, comments, and punctuation — essential for editor tooling like syntax highlighting and refactoring
CSTs enforce type safety during parsing
CSTs eliminate all parsing conflicts
questions.explanation: A CST (concrete syntax tree) retains every token from the source, including whitespace, comments, parentheses, and semicolons. An AST drops these because the compiler doesn't need them. But for editors — syntax highlighting, code folding, precise rename — you need the exact source structure, which is why tree-sitter produces CSTs.

questions.question: How does LALRPOP differ from Bison in terms of type safety?
options: LALRPOP generates C code, Bison generates Rust code — the types are language-level
LALRPOP checks that grammar actions return the correct Rust types at generation time; Bison actions are untyped C code that may fail at runtime
LALRPOP uses dynamic typing, Bison uses static typing
There is no difference — both have the same type system
questions.explanation: LALRPOP grammar rules are annotated with Rust types (e.g., `Expr: i32`). The generator verifies that all productions for a non-terminal produce the same type, and that action expressions type-check. This catches errors at generation time. Bison actions are C code snippets — type mismatches appear as runtime errors or C compiler warnings.

questions.question: In the dangling-else grammar, after parsing `IF expr THEN stmt` and seeing `ELSE`, what does Bison do by default?
options: Reduce `IF expr THEN stmt` to `stmt`, so the `else` belongs to an outer `if`
Shift the `ELSE` token, so the `else` binds to the nearest `if`
Report a compile error and refuse to generate a parser
Fork the parser into two paths (GLR mode) and explore both
questions.explanation: Bison's default conflict resolution is shift-over-reduce. So when the parser has completed `IF expr THEN stmt` and sees `ELSE`, it shifts the `ELSE`, binding it to the innermost `if`. This produces the intuitively correct behavior: `if a then if b then c else d` is parsed as `if a then (if b then c else d)`.

questions.question: What does a parser generator like Bison produce from a grammar specification?
options: A lexer
A parser (usually a shift-reduce or LR parser) implemented as a state machine with action tables
An AST directly from source code
An optimized executable
questions.explanation: Bison reads a grammar file with embedded actions and generates a parser — typically an LALR(1) parser implemented as a table-driven shift-reduce automaton. The generated parser reads tokens from the lexer, shifts them onto a stack, and reduces by grammar rules to build an AST.
